items.myth: XGBoost and LightGBM are completely different algorithms, not both gradient boosting.
items.reality: Both implement gradient boosted decision trees — same sequential residual-fitting framework. They differ in engineering: histogram details, default growth policy, sampling tricks (GOSS/EFB), and API defaults.
items.example: Same pseudo-residual regression tree inside each round; LightGBM adds leaf-wise expansion and histogram-first training.

items.myth: Histogram training is only a LightGBM feature.
items.reality: Histogram (approximate) split finding is used by both; LightGBM is histogram-native, while XGBoost exposes it via tree_method='hist' or 'approx'.
items.example: XGBClassifier(tree_method='hist') bins features similarly in spirit to LightGBM max_bin — bin count and tie-breaking differ by implementation.

items.myth: Missing values are always handled correctly if you pass NaN.
items.reality: Libraries learn a loss-optimal default branch per split on training data. That is not causal imputation and can fail when missingness carries information.
items.example: Optional survey field missing for rushed users — default branch may under-predict churn; compare with explicit imputation + indicator.

items.myth: More trees always means a better model.
items.reality: With a fixed learning rate, extra trees after the validation optimum add variance. Early stopping or lower learning_rate with more trees is the standard fix.
items.example: best_iteration=210 but n_estimators=1000 — predictions should use iteration 210, not the full 1000.

items.myth: LightGBM is always faster and better than XGBoost.
items.reality: On many large tabular sets LightGBM wins wall-clock; on smaller data or with careful tuning the gap narrows and either can win. Always benchmark on your validation protocol.
items.example: 10k-row dataset: both CV RMSE within 0.001 — pick the simpler pipeline or fewer dependencies, not the leaderboard myth.
